sp500.what: A market-cap-weighted index of ~500 of the largest U.S. companies — the broadest single gauge of American equities, and what most retirement money tracks.
sp500.why: It is forward-looking: prices embed investors' collective bets on future profits, interest rates, and risk. It moves before the economic data does — though it also false-alarms.
sp500.noteworthy: Weekly moves beyond ±5% signal real stress or euphoria. A drop of 20% from the peak is a bear market — there have been 12 since 1950, averaging about −33%.

nasdaq.what: An index of the ~3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange, heavily weighted toward technology.
nasdaq.why: The cleanest read on tech-sector sentiment specifically — when it diverges from the S&P, the market is repricing tech, not the whole economy.
nasdaq.noteworthy: It fell ~78% in the 2000–02 dot-com bust and took 15 years to reclaim the peak — the benchmark case for what a burst tech bubble looks like.

vix.what: The market's expectation of S&P 500 volatility over the next 30 days, derived from option prices — the 'fear gauge.'
vix.why: It prices how much protection investors are buying; spikes mark moments when hedging demand overwhelms complacency.
vix.noteworthy: Below 15 is calm; above 30 is genuine fear; the closing-price records are ~80 (2008 and March 2020). It spends most of its life between 12 and 20.

dollar.what: The Fed's broad index of the dollar against the currencies of all major U.S. trading partners, trade-weighted.
dollar.why: A strong dollar tightens global financial conditions (much of world debt is dollar-denominated), cheapens imports, and squeezes U.S. exporters.
dollar.noteworthy: Sustained moves of 10%+ in a year are big — 2022's surge to record highs strained emerging markets; sharp weakening usually accompanies expected Fed easing.

gold.what: The dollar price of an ounce of gold, from the COMEX futures market.
gold.why: The classic hedge against currency debasement and geopolitical risk; central banks have been the marginal buyer of recent years.
gold.noteworthy: Gold roughly quadrupled from 2001–2011, went sideways for a decade, then broke out again from 2024 — sustained new highs often track deficits, real rates, and central-bank buying.

btc.what: The dollar price of Bitcoin (Coinbase), on a log scale so each gridline is 10x the one below.
btc.why: Part risk asset, part monetary hedge — it tends to amplify liquidity conditions: soaring when money is loose, crashing hardest when it tightens.
btc.noteworthy: Four boom-bust cycles since 2013, each with drawdowns of 70–85%. The log-scale slope flattening across cycles is the maturation story; a broken cycle pattern would be news.

fedfunds.what: The interest rate banks charge each other overnight — the rate the Federal Reserve directly sets to steer the economy.
fedfunds.why: Every other borrowing cost keys off it. Hiking cycles slow the economy with a 12–18 month lag; cutting cycles historically begin shortly before or during recessions.
fedfunds.noteworthy: Near-zero 2009–15 and 2020–22 were historic anomalies; the 2022–23 hiking cycle (+5pp in 16 months) was the fastest since 1980. Rapid cuts usually mean something broke.

walcl.what: Total assets held by the Federal Reserve — the size of its balance sheet, mostly Treasuries and mortgage bonds.
walcl.why: The physical footprint of quantitative easing and tightening: expansion injects liquidity into markets, runoff drains it.
walcl.noteworthy: About $0.9T before 2008, ~$4.5T after three rounds of QE, ~$9T at the 2022 peak. Direction matters more than level — pivots from runoff back to expansion have marked every stress episode.

m2_yoy.what: Year-over-year growth of M2 — cash, checking, savings, and money-market balances held by the public.
m2_yoy.why: The money supply. Rapid growth can foreshadow inflation with a long lag; contraction is rare and restrictive.
m2_yoy.noteworthy: The 2020–21 surge (+27% y/y, the fastest ever recorded) preceded the 2021–23 inflation wave; 2023's outright contraction was the first since the 1930s.

sloos.what: The net share of senior bank loan officers telling the Fed they are tightening standards on business (C&I) loans.
sloos.why: Banks choke off credit before the economy rolls over — this survey has led downturns by two to three quarters.
sloos.noteworthy: Readings above +30–40% net tightening accompanied 1990, 2001, 2008, and 2020. Sustained positive territory while the economy still looks fine is the classic early warning.

nfci.what: The Chicago Fed's weekly composite of 105 indicators of credit, leverage, and risk appetite. Zero = long-run average; positive = tighter than normal.
nfci.why: One line summarizing whether the financial system is easy or stressed — it aggregates what spreads, funding markets, and volatility are all saying.
nfci.noteworthy: It rarely crosses zero outside genuine stress: 2008 peaked near +3, COVID spiked to +0.7 in weeks. Persistent positive readings while markets look calm deserve attention.

dgs10.what: The yield the U.S. government pays to borrow for 10 years — the benchmark interest rate for the world.
dgs10.why: It anchors mortgage and corporate borrowing costs, and it rises when markets expect more inflation or growth — or worry about absorbing more Treasury debt.
dgs10.noteworthy: Moves over 0.5pp in a month are rare and consequential. The full range runs from 0.5% (2020) to 15.8% (1981); a sustained push above ~5% would strain housing and the federal interest bill.

t10y2y.what: The gap between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields — the slope of the yield curve.
t10y2y.why: When it inverts (goes negative), markets are betting the Fed will have to cut rates — historically because a downturn is coming.
t10y2y.noteworthy: Inversion has preceded every U.S. recession since the late 1970s, usually by 6–24 months, with rare false alarms (1998). The re-steepening back through zero often lands closer to the downturn itself.

hy_oas.what: The extra yield investors demand to hold junk-rated corporate bonds instead of Treasuries.
hy_oas.why: It is a real-money gauge of default fear — credit stress usually shows up here before it shows up in stocks or headlines.
hy_oas.noteworthy: Below 3pp is calm; above 5pp is stress; above 8pp is crisis territory. COVID peaked near 11pp, 2008 near 20pp.

icsa.what: How many Americans filed first-time unemployment claims each week (4-week average) — the fastest official labor-market signal.
icsa.why: Layoffs appear here within days, months before they reach the unemployment rate.
icsa.noteworthy: Sub-250K is historically healthy. Direction beats level: a sustained climb of 15–20% off the recent low has accompanied every labor-market turn.

ccsa.what: The number of Americans continuing to draw unemployment benefits week after week (continued claims).
ccsa.why: Initial claims measure layoffs; continued claims measure whether the laid-off are getting rehired. Rising continued claims with flat initial claims = a quietly freezing job market.
ccsa.noteworthy: A grind higher over months, even without a layoff spike, has preceded rising unemployment — the 'low fire, low hire' pattern.

sahm.what: How far the 3-month average unemployment rate has risen above its low from the past 12 months.
sahm.why: It converts slow-moving unemployment data into a recession trigger with a near-perfect historical record, designed to fire early in a downturn.
sahm.noteworthy: A reading of 0.50pp or more marked the start of every recession since 1970. The one modern caveat: it tripped in mid-2024 without a recession following — unusual labor-supply shifts can distort it.

quits.what: The share of workers voluntarily quitting their jobs each month (JOLTS survey).
quits.why: People quit when they're confident they can do better — quits lead wages and payrolls. A falling quits rate signals cooling before layoffs begin.
quits.noteworthy: The 2021–22 'Great Resignation' peaked at 3.0%; readings near 2.0% or below mark a cautious, low-churn labor market like 2009–13.

temphelp.what: Year-over-year change in temporary-help employment — staffing-agency workers.
temphelp.why: Temps are cut first in a slowdown and hired first in a recovery; this series turns before overall payrolls.
temphelp.noteworthy: Sustained negative territory preceded or accompanied every recession since 1991 — though it can also stay weak during structural shifts in staffing.

cpi_yoy.what: How much consumer prices rose versus a year ago; the core line strips out volatile food and energy.
cpi_yoy.why: This is THE inflation number — it sets real-wage math, Social Security adjustments, and Federal Reserve policy.
cpi_yoy.noteworthy: The Fed targets about 2% (on PCE, a related gauge that runs slightly cooler). Headline above 5% is rare outside the 1970s and 2021–23. Core is the trend; headline is the noise.

ppi_yoy.what: Year-over-year change in the prices producers receive — wholesale inflation, one step upstream of consumer prices.
ppi_yoy.why: Cost pressure shows up here before it reaches the checkout aisle; producers either absorb it (margins) or pass it on (CPI).
ppi_yoy.noteworthy: PPI led CPI on the way up in 2021 (peaking over 11%) and on the way down in 2022–23. A PPI-CPI gap that persists usually closes via consumer prices.

unrate.what: The share of the labor force actively looking for work.
unrate.why: The headline gauge of labor-market health — slow to turn, but decisive when it does.
unrate.noteworthy: Below 4% is historically tight. Recessions typically add 2–5pp. It lags the cycle — watch 3+ month direction, not single prints (the Sahm chart converts this series into an early trigger).

payems_chg.what: How many jobs the economy added or lost each month (nonfarm payrolls, first differences).
payems_chg.why: The single most market-moving statistic in America — released the first Friday of each month.
payems_chg.noteworthy: Roughly +80–100K/month keeps up with population growth; sustained prints below that mark stall speed. Revisions run large — trend beats any single bar.

lfpr_prime.what: The share of 25–54 year-olds working or looking for work — participation without the retirement-wave distortion.
lfpr_prime.why: The cleanest answer to 'are working-age Americans actually engaged in the economy?' Immune to the aging-population effects that muddy the headline rate.
lfpr_prime.noteworthy: The all-time range is narrow: ~80–84%. Long slides (2000–2015) reflected structural detachment; the post-COVID recovery to ~83%+ was a genuine surprise.

jtsjol.what: Total job openings employers report (JOLTS) — unfilled demand for labor.
jtsjol.why: Openings falling while unemployment stays low is the 'soft landing' path; openings falling with unemployment rising is the hard one.
jtsjol.noteworthy: Openings peaked near 12M in 2022 (2 per unemployed worker, an all-time record). The pre-COVID normal was ~7M; the ratio of openings to unemployed is the tightness gauge.

gdp_growth.what: The quarterly growth rate of inflation-adjusted output, annualized.
gdp_growth.why: The official scoreboard of the economy — but heavily revised and two months stale by first release.
gdp_growth.noteworthy: Long-run trend is ~2%. Two consecutive negative quarters is the rule-of-thumb recession (not the official definition). Swings beyond ±4% outside recessions/rebounds are rare.

wage_vs_cpi.what: Wage growth (average hourly earnings) plotted against inflation (CPI), both year-over-year.
wage_vs_cpi.why: The gap between the lines is real purchasing power: wages above inflation = living standards rising; below = a pay cut in real terms.
wage_vs_cpi.noteworthy: Inflation outran wages for most of 2021–23 — the core of that era's economic discontent — before the gap flipped positive again in late 2023.

umcsent.what: The University of Michigan's monthly survey of how consumers feel about their finances and the economy.
umcsent.why: Sentiment drives spending, and spending is two-thirds of GDP. It also captures inflation's psychological bite better than most hard data.
umcsent.noteworthy: The 2022 reading of 50 was the survey's all-time low — below 2008 — driven by inflation. Readings above 90 marked the strong late-90s and 2019 economies. Partisan gaps are large; the average is what matters.

retail_yoy.what: Year-over-year change in retail sales, adjusted for inflation — real volumes, not just higher prices.
retail_yoy.why: The consumer is the U.S. economy's engine; real retail shows whether households are actually buying more or just paying more.
retail_yoy.noteworthy: Nominal sales grew through 2022 while real sales shrank — the inflation illusion this chart exists to strip out. Sustained real declines accompany consumer recessions.

indpro_yoy.what: Year-over-year change in the physical output of factories, mines, and utilities.
indpro_yoy.why: Manufacturing is a small share of GDP now but still the most cyclical — industrial downturns lead broad ones more often than not.
indpro_yoy.noteworthy: Sustained negative territory outside a recession has happened (2015–16, 2019) — 'industrial recessions' the services economy powered through. Deep negatives (-5%+) mean the real thing.

t5yie.what: The inflation rate bond markets expect to average over the next five years, from the TIPS-Treasury spread.
t5yie.why: Expectations drive behavior: if markets stop believing in ~2% inflation, the Fed's job gets much harder and rates stay higher for longer.
t5yie.noteworthy: Anchored means 1.5–2.5%. The 2022 spike above 3.5% was the 'de-anchoring' scare; a drop below 1% (2008, 2020) signals deflation fear.

debt.what: Total federal debt outstanding, updated daily by the Treasury.
debt.why: The accumulated stock the deficit flows into. Interest on it now rivals defense as a budget line, which feeds back into the deficit itself.
debt.noteworthy: Gross debt crossed 100% of GDP around 2013 and kept going. Recent pace: roughly $1T added every five to six months.

debt_gdp.what: Federal debt as a share of the economy — the standard measure of debt burden across time and countries.
debt_gdp.why: Debt only means something relative to the income that services it; this is the ratio fiscal sustainability debates are actually about.
debt_gdp.noteworthy: The WWII peak was ~119%; the post-2020 era sits in that territory permanently, in peacetime. Academic warning zones start around 90–100%, though rates matter as much as levels.

deficit12.what: The gap between federal spending and revenue over the trailing 12 months.
deficit12.why: This is the pace of new borrowing — the flow that feeds the debt. Deficits this large outside war or recession are historically novel.
deficit12.noteworthy: Roughly $900B (~3% of GDP) was the pre-2020 norm; a sustained ~$2T deficit at full employment is the current anomaly. Watch the trend through tax season (April) swings.

interest.what: What the federal government pays annually in interest on its debt (annualized quarterly rate).
interest.why: The compounding cost of past deficits — every dollar here is spent before Congress votes on anything. It crowds the rest of the budget.
interest.noteworthy: Interest costs roughly doubled from 2021 to 2025, passing $1T/year — exceeding the defense budget. The ratio to tax revenue (~20%) is the sustainability number to watch.

cs_level.what: The Case-Shiller national index level — repeat-sales prices of the same homes, the gold standard (2-month lag).
cs_level.why: The long view of the biggest asset most families own; the level shows cumulative appreciation across decades.
cs_level.noteworthy: National prices roughly doubled 2012–2022 and did NOT give back the 2021–22 surge — unlike 2007–11, when the index fell 27% peak-to-trough.

cs_yoy.what: Year-over-year change in prices of the same homes selling twice (Case-Shiller repeat-sales method — the gold standard, published with a ~2-month lag).
cs_yoy.why: Housing is most households' largest asset and purchase; price momentum drives wealth effects, affordability, and construction.
cs_yoy.noteworthy: The long-run norm is about +3–5% a year. Above +10% is boom territory (2005, 2021). Sustained negative prints have one modern precedent: 2007–11.

mspus.what: The median sale price of homes sold each quarter (Census/HUD).
mspus.why: The sticker price a buyer actually faces — though it shifts with the mix of what's selling, not just appreciation.
mspus.noteworthy: Crossed $400K for the first time in 2021. Mix effects can move it when Case-Shiller is flat — divergence between the two is itself information.

mortgage30.what: The average rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage (Freddie Mac weekly survey).
mortgage30.why: The single number that sets housing affordability — each +1pp cuts a buyer's budget roughly 10%. It also locks in owners ('golden handcuffs') and freezes inventory.
mortgage30.noteworthy: The 2021 low of 2.65% was the lowest ever recorded; the 2023 peak near 8% was a 23-year high. The historical norm is 6–8%; 1981 hit 18%.

houst.what: How many new housing units broke ground each month (annualized).
houst.why: Construction is the economy's most interest-rate-sensitive sector — housing starts lead recessions and recoveries.
houst.noteworthy: The 50-year range: ~2.3M at the 1972/2006 peaks, 478K at the 2009 trough. America has underbuilt relative to household formation since 2008 — the structural shortage behind high prices.

permit.what: Building permits issued for new housing units — starts, one step earlier.
permit.why: Nobody pulls a permit they don't intend to use; this is the construction pipeline's leading edge.
permit.noteworthy: Permits rolling over ahead of starts has flagged every housing downturn; watch the gap between permits and starts for builder hesitation.

msacsr.what: How many months it would take to sell every new home on the market at the current sales pace.
msacsr.why: The supply-demand balance for new construction: low = sellers' market, high = builders stuck with inventory.
msacsr.noteworthy: 4–6 months is balanced. Above 8–9 (2008, briefly 2022) forces builder price cuts; below 4 (2020–21) means bidding wars.

zhvi.what: Zillow's estimate of the typical U.S. home value, monthly and fresher than Case-Shiller.
zhvi.why: The timeliest broad home-price gauge — useful for catching turns a month or two before the official indexes confirm them.
zhvi.noteworthy: When ZHVI and Case-Shiller disagree on direction, ZHVI is usually early rather than wrong — but it is a model estimate, not recorded sales.

zori.what: Zillow's index of asking rents across the U.S.
zori.why: Rent is the biggest single line in CPI (with a ~year lag from asking rents) and the housing cost renters actually face now.
zori.noteworthy: Asking rents jumped ~15% in 2021–22 — the pipeline that kept CPI shelter inflation hot into 2023–24. Flat-to-falling ZORI is the leading edge of shelter disinflation.

delinq.what: The share of single-family mortgages at commercial banks that are past due.
delinq.why: The canary for housing-driven financial stress — 2008's crisis was, at root, this line going vertical.
delinq.noteworthy: Post-2015 norms are historically low (1.5–3%); the 2010 peak was ~11%. A sustained climb from the floor matters more than the level.

inventory.what: Active for-sale listings nationwide (Realtor.com; series begins 2016).
inventory.why: Inventory turns before prices do. Rising listings with slowing sales is how every housing correction announces itself.
inventory.noteworthy: The 2022 trough (~350K listings) was the tightest market ever measured; pre-COVID normal was ~1.2M. Recovery toward normal supply is the precondition for a balanced market.

wti.what: The benchmark price of U.S. crude oil (West Texas Intermediate).
wti.why: Oil is the economy's tax and geopolitics' thermometer — spikes hit consumers within weeks and have preceded most recessions since 1973.
wti.noteworthy: $60–80 is the modern comfort zone. Sustained moves above $100 (2008, 2011–14, 2022) strain consumers; a collapse below $40 signals demand collapse or supply war.

gas.what: The average U.S. price of regular gasoline (EIA weekly).
gas.why: The most visible price in America — it shapes inflation psychology and consumer sentiment far beyond its budget share.
gas.noteworthy: The June 2022 record was $5.01; below $3 reads as cheap in the post-COVID era. It tracks oil with a few weeks' lag, plus refinery events.

natgas.what: The benchmark U.S. natural gas price (Henry Hub).
natgas.why: Sets electricity costs and heating bills, and — via LNG exports — links American prices to European and Asian crises.
natgas.noteworthy: $2–4 is normal; the 2022 spike to $9+ reflected Europe's post-Ukraine scramble. U.S. prices stayed an order of magnitude below Europe's — the shale advantage.

elec.what: The average residential electricity price (BLS, per kWh).
elec.why: The grid bill everyone pays — and increasingly the meeting point of AI datacenter demand, electrification, and generation constraints.
elec.noteworthy: Electricity inflation historically ran below CPI; it has outpaced it since 2021. Sustained acceleration here is the consumer-facing edge of the grid-demand story.

crude_prod.what: How much crude oil the U.S. pumps per day (EIA weekly).
crude_prod.why: The shale revolution made the U.S. the world's largest producer — this line is American energy leverage in one number.
crude_prod.noteworthy: ~5M bbl/day in 2008 to a record ~13.5M by 2024–25. Plateaus at record highs despite price signals suggest the shale growth era maturing.

swb_fy.what: Border Patrol apprehensions between ports of entry on the southwest border, per federal fiscal year (Oct–Sep).
swb_fy.why: The longest-running consistent gauge of illegal-crossing volume — monthly numbers make headlines, but this series shows the regime shifts.
swb_fy.noteworthy: The 1986 and 2000 waves peaked near 1.6M. The 2017 modern low was 304K. FY2021–23 set all-time records above 2M. FY2025 came in at 238K — the lowest since 1970.

border_monthly.what: All CBP encounters each month — nationwide and for the Southwest land border specifically. Counts Border Patrol apprehensions between ports of entry plus Office of Field Operations inadmissibles at ports (Title 8), and Title 42 expulsions through May 2023, across every demographic and nationality.
border_monthly.why: Monthly cadence exposes turning points an annual total hides — policy changes, seasonal patterns, and surges or drops register here first. The nationwide-versus-Southwest split shows how much of the flow is at the SW border versus everywhere else.
border_monthly.noteworthy: Nationwide monthly encounters peaked near 371K in December 2023, an all-time high, with the Southwest land border the large majority of it. Both fell steeply through 2024–25 to monthly lows near 25K nationwide by mid-2025 — levels not seen in over a decade — running near 33K into early 2026.

murder_rate.what: Homicides per 100,000 people, from FBI annual national estimates.
murder_rate.why: The best-measured crime statistic (bodies get counted; lesser crimes go unreported at varying rates) — the cleanest proxy for violent-crime trends.
murder_rate.noteworthy: The 1980 peak hit 10.2; the 1990s decline cut it roughly in half. 2020 saw the largest one-year jump on record (~+29%), which fully reversed by 2024 (~5.0).

rtci_ytd.what: Year-to-date crime counts vs. the same period last year, from 300+ city police departments reporting in near-real-time (AH Datalytics sample).
rtci_ytd.why: Official FBI numbers arrive a year late; this sample is the only way to see crime trends as they happen.
rtci_ytd.noteworthy: It's provisional and city-skewed, but it correctly tracked the 2023–25 murder decline a year before official data confirmed it. Treat ±2–3% as noise; ±10%+ as a real trend.

overdoses.what: Deaths from drug overdoses over each trailing 12 months (CDC provisional, ~6-month lag).
overdoses.why: The deadliest drug epidemic in U.S. history, driven since 2015 by illicit fentanyl — a public-health series with border, crime, and social dimensions.
overdoses.noteworthy: The 2023 peak was ~112K/year — more than car crashes and guns combined. The decline that began in 2024 (fastest drop ever recorded) is the trend to watch: durable or pause?

fertility.what: The number of children the average woman would have over her lifetime at current birth rates.
fertility.why: Below ~2.1, population shrinks without immigration. Today's number sets school enrollment, workforce size, and entitlement solvency decades ahead.
fertility.noteworthy: The U.S. fell below replacement in 2008 and never recovered; 1.60 (2024) is the record low. Most developed peers are lower still — South Korea is under 0.8.

life_exp.what: How long a newborn would live at current death rates — the summary statistic of national health.
life_exp.why: It compresses everything — disease, drugs, violence, health care — into one number, and the U.S. runs 4–6 years behind peer countries.
life_exp.noteworthy: COVID erased 2.4 years (78.8 → 76.4), the largest drop since WWII; recovery to 79.0 by 2024 finally passed the 2019 level. The stagnation from 2010–2019 (drugs, obesity) was itself historically unusual.

marriage.why: Family formation upstream of fertility, household economics, and much social policy — a slow variable that moves everything else.
marriage.noteworthy: From 10.6 (1980) to ~6 today — a 40% decline. The 2020 COVID trough (5.1) mostly rebounded; the long slide did not.

trust_media.what: The share of Americans telling Gallup they have a great deal or fair amount of trust in mass media to report the news fully, accurately, and fairly.
trust_media.why: A proxy for whether the country still has a shared factual baseline — and for the audience migration to alternative information sources.
trust_media.noteworthy: Trust peaked at 72% in 1976 (post-Watergate) and hit 28% in 2025. The decline spans all groups but is deepest among Republicans (8%) and independents (27%).

church.what: The share of Americans belonging to a church, synagogue, or mosque (Gallup; last major reading 2020).
church.why: Religious affiliation anchors community, charity, and social capital — its decline is one of the largest quiet shifts in American life.
church.noteworthy: Stable near 70% for six decades, then a 20-point fall in 20 years to 47% by 2020 — the first minority reading in Gallup's 80-year series.

approval.what: The president's approval and disapproval ratings — an unweighted 14-day trailing average of all public polls (VoteHub).
approval.why: The daily scoreboard of political capital: it shapes what a president can pass, how parties behave, and midterm outcomes.
approval.noteworthy: Modern presidents rarely sustain approval above 55% or below 37%. Net approval around −20 historically precedes midterm losses for the president's party; watch the trend, not any single week.

midterm_odds.what: Prediction-market prices for Democratic control of the House and Senate after the November 2026 midterms (Polymarket).
midterm_odds.why: Real-money odds aggregate polls, fundamentals, and news faster than any single forecaster — the closest thing to a live consensus forecast.
midterm_odds.noteworthy: Prices are probabilities, not predictions: 80 means 4-in-5 — the 20 happens. Sharp moves without news suggest big-money positioning; the 2026 series will converge toward 0 or 100 by election night.

ai_compute.what: The computing power (FLOP) used to train each notable AI model — one dot per model, on a log scale where each gridline is 10x the one below.
ai_compute.why: Training compute has been the strongest single predictor of AI capability. The slope of the top edge IS the pace of the AI race.
ai_compute.noteworthy: Frontier training compute has grown roughly 4–5x per year since 2010 — about 10,000x per decade. A flattening of the top edge would signal a genuine regime change in AI progress.

ai_releases.what: How many notable AI models were released each quarter (Epoch AI's curated list).
ai_releases.why: The cadence of the AI race — acceleration means more labs at the frontier; deceleration could mean consolidation or hitting limits.
ai_releases.noteworthy: From a handful per quarter in the 2010s to dozens post-2023. A sustained drop while compute keeps scaling would suggest consolidation into fewer, bigger models.

sox.what: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index — the 30 leading chip companies.
sox.why: Chips are the substrate of the AI buildout and the economy's most cyclical tech sector; the SOX prices both the AI boom and its financing conditions.
sox.noteworthy: It fell ~85% in 2000–02 — the case study for infrastructure-boom repricing. Sharp SOX underperformance vs. the Nasdaq is an early crack signal for the AI trade.

datacenter.what: Monthly U.S. spending on data-center construction (Census, via Our World in Data).
datacenter.why: The physical footprint of the AI investment boom — concrete and power, not press releases. Capex commitments show conviction better than announcements.
datacenter.noteworthy: Spending roughly tripled from 2022 to 2025 (past $2B/month). A rollover here would be the first hard evidence of the AI buildout cooling.

usdcny.what: How many Chinese yuan one dollar buys.
usdcny.why: The managed exchange rate at the center of U.S.-China economic competition — Beijing weakens it to support exports, defends it to prevent capital flight.
usdcny.noteworthy: 7.0 was the psychological line Beijing defended for years; sustained moves past 7.3 signal either deliberate devaluation or capital pressure. Sharp strengthening accompanies détente or dollar weakness.

china_trade.what: Monthly U.S. goods imports from and exports to China.
china_trade.why: The decoupling scoreboard: tariffs, re-shoring, and trans-shipment all show up here before they show up in speeches.
china_trade.noteworthy: Imports peaked near $50B/month in 2022; the decline since is the real measure of decoupling — though some trade re-routes through Vietnam and Mexico rather than disappearing.

gpr.what: The Geopolitical Risk index — counting war- and tension-related coverage across major newspapers since 1985 (Caldara-Iacoviello).
gpr.why: A quantified read on how close the world is to conflict — spikes precede oil shocks, defense buildups, and risk-off markets.
gpr.noteworthy: 9/11 and the Iraq invasion set the records (300+); Ukraine 2022 hit ~180. A sustained level shift — not just spikes — marks a genuinely more dangerous world.

gscpi.what: The NY Fed's global supply chain pressure index — shipping costs and delivery times worldwide, in standard deviations from normal.
gscpi.why: Supply-chain stress shows up here months before it reaches goods prices — it explained much of the 2021–22 inflation surge.
gscpi.noteworthy: The 2021 peak (+4.3σ) was the most stressed reading ever constructed; negative values mean looser-than-normal chains. A renewed spike would be an early inflation warning.
